text: This is a case of laparoscopic management of ovarian torsion in a young eighteen year old unmarried girl. The patient profile was as follows. This patient was an eighteen year old unmarried girl with chief complaints of pain in the abdomen with frequent acute exacerbations since fifteen days. There was a past history of right ovarian torsion two years ago which had been managed conservatively without surgery. Present ultrasound showed torsion of the left ovary with an eleven centimeter tubo-ovarian mass. On CT scan, a large twelve centimeters enlarged ovary was seen however there was no specific cyst delineated within the ovary. The expected pathology hence was left at nexal torsion. These are the CT scan images and the uterus is seen along with the mass which is of variegated consistency and ecotexture seen to be filling up the entire pelvis. The surgical dilemmas before starting the case were that if no cyst is seen and the enlargement is purely due to ovarian edema, should the concerned ovary be left untouched after detorsion and can it undergo torsion again later due to its increased size. The second dilemma of course was the possibility of oophorectomy being explained to an eighteen year old girl specifically because she had a fifteen day old history of an ovarian torsion.

The guiding surgical principles to be remembered before starting the surgery were to identify and restore the anatomy, to do a careful adhesiolysis because the tissue was expected to be edematous and friable, to preserve the ovary at all costs unless absolutely necessary to remove it due to necrosis, to avoid cautery as far as possible and to ensure that repeat torsion does not occur. of the fact that the this patient had not undergone any previous surgery or did not have a history of pelvic tuberculosis a significant amount of adhesions were noted in the pelvis these were probably reactive adhesions due to inflammation and hence were flimsy at some places but were quite hard and difficult to dissect at other places What is important to remember is that the surrounding tissue was largely edematous and it was very important to make a distinction between normal tissue and edematous tissue before proceeding with the dissection. Particularly identification of structures was going to be very important before dissecting and separating them out one from the other. But firm traction on the tissues was able to yield the correct plane of dissection most of the times. In such cases it is very important not to go and start off dissecting with an energy source or with a cold scissors because the plane of dissection may be obscured.

The surgeon performs slow and steady blunt dissection to separate the small bowel from densely adhered adnexal structures, revealing the ovary and tube beneath. The suction cannula is used to assist in blunt dissection and clear the surgical field. As flimsy adhesions give way, the uterus and ovarian mass become clearly visible, restoring the pelvis to a more normal structure.

The surgeon uses gentle blunt dissection to separate the ovarian mass from adhesions on the right adnexa, focusing on the posterior surface of the uterus. The suction cannula aids in providing traction and clearing the surgical field, allowing the ovarian mass to be delineated from the uterus. The round ligament and fimbria of the fallopian tube are identified and separated from the ovarian mass using blunt dissection. The left ovary is pulled to the left side, and the suction cannula is used to dissect between the ovary and posterior structures, including the bowel. Both ovaries are identified, and blood is cleared from the pouch of Douglas to maintain visibility.

The surgeon separates the right and left ovaries, which are adherent to each other in the midline, and identifies the fallopian tube adherent to the left ovary. The adnexa are completely separated, allowing the surgeon to proceed with the left-sided ovarian cystectomy. The sigmoid colon is dissected from the lateral pelvic wall to create more space in the pelvis, facilitating the detorsion of the ovary. The left tube and ovary, which have undergone torsion, are visible, with the ovary showing signs of viability.

The surgeon identifies the pelvic structures and proceeds with detorsion of the left ovary by gently lifting the uterus and twisting the ovary to untwist it. The ovary and fallopian tube are lifted away from the pelvis, and the ovary is untwisted after three turns. Adhesions between the tube, ovary, and round ligament are identified and separated using blunt dissection. The infundibulopelvic and utero-ovarian ligaments are visible and do not require dissection. Remaining adhesions between the ovary and posterior peritoneum are separated, allowing clear visualization of the ovary, which is now ready for ovarian cystectomy.

The surgeon identifies a hemorrhagic ovarian cyst within the ovary and performs a cystectomy. The excised cyst wall is placed inside a modified endo bag for removal from the abdomen, ensuring no contact with the abdominal wall. The ovary is then reconstructed using a vicryl suture to approximate the raw edges, minimizing postoperative adhesions without the need for cautery.

The surgeon uses the 'hot dog in double bun' technique to prevent ovarian torsion, anchoring the ovary to the round ligament without compromising the fallopian tube. Sutures are passed from the ovary under the fallopian tube and through the round ligament, ensuring the ovary remains fixed without kinking the tube. The ovary returns to a healthy color, indicating no loss of viability, and irrigation confirms no bleeding. The right ovary is similarly fixed using the same technique.
